items.title: Dissolution by death of a Partner where only Two individual partners are in firm
items.summary: The article explains that a partnership comprising only two partners stands dissolved on the death of one partner under the Partnership Act. It also outlines the tax and compliance implications of carrying on the business until a new firm is constituted. The post Dissolution by death of a Partner where only Two individual partners are in firm appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Partnership Act, 1932, governs the formation, management, and dissolution of partnership firms in India. According to Section 42 of the Partnership Act, the death of a partner results in the dissolution of the partnership, unless there is a contract to the contrary. In the context of income tax, the dissolution of a partnership firm has significant implications, particularly with regard to the assessment and taxation of the firm's income. Section 188 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides that the tax liability of a firm is joint and several, meaning that all partners are liable for the tax dues of the firm. Furthermore, Section 189 of the Income Tax Act deals with the tax implications of the dissolution of a partnership firm, including the assessment of the firm's income and the allocation of tax liability among the partners.

The Partnership Act and the Income Tax Act must be read together to understand the legal and tax implications of the dissolution of a partnership firm. The Supreme Court has also clarified the position in this regard in the case of Commissioner of Income Tax vs. M. Kumaraswamy, where it was held that the death of a partner results in the dissolution of the partnership, unless there is a contract to the contrary. This judgment has significant implications for the assessment and taxation of partnership firms, particularly in cases where there are only two individual partners.

In the context of a partnership comprising only two individual partners, the death of one partner results in the automatic dissolution of the partnership, unless there is a contract to the contrary. This is because the Partnership Act does not provide for the continuation of a partnership with only one partner. In such cases, the tax implications of the dissolution of the partnership firm must be carefully considered, including the assessment of the firm's income and the allocation of tax liability among the partners. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the tax implications of the dissolution of partnership firms, particularly in cases where there are only two individual partners.

The Development in Detail

The development in question relates to the dissolution of a partnership firm comprising only two individual partners, where one partner has died. In such cases, the partnership stands dissolved, and the business cannot be carried on in the same name and style. However, the surviving partner may continue to carry on the business until a new firm is constituted, subject to certain conditions and tax implications. The tax implications of carrying on the business until a new firm is constituted are significant, particularly with regard to the assessment and taxation of the firm's income.

According to Section 9(1) of the Income Tax Act, the income of a partnership firm is taxable in the hands of the firm, and not in the hands of the individual partners. However, in cases where the partnership firm is dissolved, the tax liability of the firm is apportioned among the partners in accordance with Section 189 of the Income Tax Act. The CBDT has also issued guidelines to clarify the tax implications of the dissolution of partnership firms, particularly in cases where there are only two individual partners. For example, Circular No. 36/2016 dated October 25, 2016, provides guidance on the tax implications of the dissolution of partnership firms, including the assessment of the firm's income and the allocation of tax liability among the partners.

The development in question highlights the importance of careful tax planning and compliance in cases where a partnership firm is dissolved due to the death of a partner. The surviving partner must ensure that all tax dues of the firm are paid, and that the firm's income is assessed and taxed in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act. The CBDT has also emphasized the need for strict compliance with tax laws and regulations, particularly in cases where partnership firms are dissolved due to the death of a partner.

The impact of the development in question is significant, particularly with regard to the assessment and taxation of partnership firms. The dissolution of a partnership firm due to the death of a partner results in the automatic dissolution of the partnership, unless there is a contract to the contrary. The CBDT has also emphasized the need for strict compliance with tax laws and regulations, particularly in cases where partnership firms are dissolved due to the death of a partner.

items.title: India opens door wider for Foreign Nationals to access Indian Stock Market
items.summary: India has expanded portfolio investment access by allowing any individual resident outside India to invest in listed Indian companies. The amendment widens the investor base while retaining safeguards for sensitive investments and ownership changes. The post India opens door wider for Foreign Nationals to access Indian Stock Market appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian government has consistently sought to liberalize and deepen its capital markets, recognizing the critical role that foreign investment plays in fueling economic growth. This endeavor is reflected in various provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, particularly those related to the taxation of foreign-sourced income and the treatment of non-resident investors. Section 9(1) of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the income deemed to accrue or arise in India, is a cornerstone in understanding the tax implications for foreign nationals investing in the Indian stock market. Furthermore, the regulations under the Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (FEMA), administered by the Reserve Bank of India, provide the overarching framework for foreign investment in India, including portfolio investments in listed companies.

Historically, the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) has played a pivotal role in regulating and facilitating foreign investment in the Indian securities market. The SEBI (Foreign Portfolio Investors) Regulations, 2019, for instance, outline the requirements and procedures for foreign portfolio investors seeking to invest in Indian securities. These regulations, coupled with the income tax laws, form the basis for understanding the legal and regulatory environment governing foreign investment in India. The recent amendment expanding portfolio investment access to any individual resident outside India is a significant development within this framework.

The legal reasoning behind such amendments often involves a delicate balance between attracting foreign capital and protecting national interests, including sensitive sectors and industries. The government's approach is guided by the need to ensure that while foreign investment is encouraged, it does not compromise national security or the competitive position of domestic industries. This balance is reflected in the specific provisions and safeguards that are built into the regulatory framework governing foreign investment, including the requirement for prior approvals in certain sectors and the stipulation of ownership limits.

The Development in Detail

The latest development allowing any individual resident outside India to invest in listed Indian companies marks a significant step in the liberalization of India's capital account. This move is expected to widen the investor base for Indian companies, potentially leading to increased foreign portfolio investment and deeper integration of Indian markets with global financial markets. The amendment is part of the government's broader strategy to improve the ease of doing business in India and to make the country a more attractive destination for foreign investors.

The specifics of the amendment, including the conditions and requirements that foreign individual investors must meet, will be crucial in determining its impact. For instance, the amendment may stipulate certain disclosure requirements, investment limits, or registration processes that foreign individual investors must comply with. These details will be key to understanding how the amendment will operate in practice and what implications it may have for both foreign investors and Indian companies seeking to access foreign capital.

The development is also noteworthy because it reflects the government's willingness to adapt and refine its policies in response to changing economic conditions and the evolving needs of the financial sector. The ability to attract and retain foreign investment is critical for supporting economic growth, and the government's moves in this area will be closely watched by investors, businesses, and policymakers alike. The amendment's success will depend on its ability to strike the right balance between openness to foreign investment and the protection of domestic interests.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the amendment are expected to include simplified registration and investment procedures for foreign individual investors, potentially streamlined through online platforms. Additionally, the amendment may clarify the tax treatment of investments made by foreign individuals, providing certainty on issues such as withholding tax rates and the availability of tax credits. These provisions will be critical in reducing barriers to entry and enhancing the overall investment experience for foreign investors.

The impact of the amendment is likely to be multifaceted. On one hand, it could lead to an increase in foreign portfolio investment, which could contribute to deeper and more liquid capital markets in India. This, in turn, could support the growth of Indian companies by providing them with access to a broader pool of capital. On the other hand, the amendment may also introduce new regulatory challenges, particularly in terms of monitoring and enforcing compliance with the stipulated conditions and requirements.

From a legal standpoint, the amendment will need to be harmonized with existing laws and regulations, including the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the FEMA regulations. This will involve ensuring that the tax implications of investments made by foreign individuals are clearly understood and that the regulatory framework is cohesive and effective. The role of the CBDT and the CBIC in providing clarifications and guidelines on the tax aspects of the amendment will be particularly important in this regard.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the amendment reveals both opportunities and challenges. On the positive side, the move has the potential to attract significant foreign investment, which could bolster the Indian economy and support the growth of domestic companies. The simplification of investment procedures and the clarification of tax treatment could make India a more competitive destination for foreign investors, potentially leading to increased economic activity and job creation.

However, there are also potential risks and challenges associated with the amendment. For instance, the increased openness to foreign investment may introduce new vulnerabilities, particularly in terms of economic stability and national security. The government will need to carefully monitor the impact of the amendment and be prepared to respond to any unintended consequences that may arise.

From a legal perspective, the amendment highlights the importance of having a clear and coherent regulatory framework that balances the need to attract foreign investment with the need to protect domestic interests. The role of the judiciary in interpreting the laws and regulations governing foreign investment will also be crucial, particularly in resolving any disputes that may arise and in providing clarity on the application of the law.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

For practitioners, including chartered accountants and tax lawyers, the amendment presents several key action points. Firstly, it will be essential to closely monitor the regulatory developments and updates issued by the government and regulatory bodies, including the CBDT and the CBIC. This will involve staying abreast of any new guidelines, circulars, or notifications that may be issued to clarify the operation of the amendment.

Secondly, practitioners will need to advise their clients on the implications of the amendment, including the potential benefits and risks. This will involve providing guidance on the registration and investment procedures, as well as the tax treatment of investments made by foreign individuals. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. **Monitor Regulatory Updates**: Keep track of any new regulations, guidelines, or circulars issued by the government or regulatory bodies regarding the amendment.

2. **Advise on Investment Procedures**: Guide clients on the simplified registration and investment procedures for foreign individual investors, ensuring compliance with all stipulated conditions and requirements.

3. **Clarify Tax Implications**: Provide clarity on the tax treatment of investments made by foreign individuals, including withholding tax rates and the availability of tax credits.

4. **Conduct Risk Assessments**: Advise clients on the potential risks and challenges associated with the amendment, including economic stability and national security concerns.

5. **Ensure Compliance**: Assist clients in ensuring compliance with all regulatory requirements, including disclosure obligations and investment limits, to avoid any legal or regulatory issues.

items.title: Rejected Books Cannot Sustain Section 271DA Penalty: Hyderabad ITAT
items.summary: The ITAT held that penalty under Section 271DA cannot be sustained where the Assessing Officer failed to record a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order. The ruling reiterates that such satisfaction is a mandatory jurisdictional requirement. The post Rejected Books Cannot Sustain Section 271DA Penalty: Hyderabad ITAT appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, is replete with provisions aimed at curbing tax evasion and ensuring compliance with tax laws. One such provision is Section 269ST, which prohibits a person from receiving an amount of two lakh rupees or more otherwise than by an account payee cheque or an account payee bank draft or use of electronic clearing system through a bank account. The violation of this provision attracts a penalty under Section 271DA, which can be levied by the Assessing Officer. However, the imposition of such penalty is not automatic and requires the Assessing Officer to record a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order.

Section 271DA of the Income Tax Act provides that if a person receives any sum in contravention of the provisions of Section 269ST, he shall be liable to pay a penalty, which shall be equal to the amount of such sum received. The section also provides that any such penalty shall be imposed by the Joint Commissioner or the Assistant Commissioner or the Deputy Commissioner. The provision is a clear indication of the legislative intent to curb the menace of unaccounted cash transactions and to promote digital payments.

The concept of "satisfaction" is a crucial aspect of the penalty provisions under the Income Tax Act. The Assessing Officer must be satisfied that the conditions for imposing the penalty are met, and this satisfaction must be recorded in the assessment order. The recording of such satisfaction is a mandatory jurisdictional requirement, and the failure to do so would render the penalty order invalid. This principle has been reiterated by the courts in various decisions, emphasizing the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements while imposing penalties under the Act.

Facts of the Case

The present case before the Hyderabad ITAT involved the imposition of a penalty under Section 271DA for receiving an amount in contravention of Section 269ST. The Assessing Officer had rejected the books of accounts of the assessee and had levied the penalty for receiving cash exceeding the prescribed limit. The assessee had challenged the penalty order before the Tribunal, contending that the Assessing Officer had failed to record a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order.

The assessee had also argued that the rejection of the books of accounts was not a sufficient ground for imposing the penalty under Section 271DA. The assessee had maintained that the penalty provisions under the Act are strict and require strict compliance with the procedural requirements. The assessee had also relied on various judicial precedents, which have held that the imposition of penalty is not automatic and requires a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of the relevant provision.

The revenue authorities had, however, contended that the rejection of the books of accounts was sufficient to impose the penalty under Section 271DA. They had argued that the Assessing Officer had recorded a satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST, although it was not explicitly stated in the assessment order. The revenue authorities had also relied on various provisions of the Act, which provide for the imposition of penalty in cases of contravention of the provisions of the Act.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the Tribunal was whether the penalty under Section 271DA could be sustained in the absence of a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order. The Tribunal had to consider the provisions of Section 271DA and the relevant judicial precedents to determine whether the imposition of penalty was valid. The Tribunal also had to examine the facts of the case and the arguments advanced by the parties to decide the issue.

The Tribunal had to consider the question of whether the rejection of the books of accounts was sufficient to impose the penalty under Section 271DA. The Tribunal had to examine the provisions of the Act and the relevant judicial precedents to determine whether the imposition of penalty was automatic or required a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST. The Tribunal also had to consider the arguments advanced by the parties and the evidence on record to decide the issue.

The issue before the Tribunal was of great importance, as it involved the interpretation of the penalty provisions under the Act. The decision of the Tribunal would have a significant impact on the assessee and would also provide guidance to the tax authorities and the assessees on the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA. The Tribunal's decision would also contribute to the development of the law on the subject and would provide clarity on the requirements for imposing penalty under the Act.

Arguments & Contentions

The assessee had argued that the penalty under Section 271DA could not be imposed without a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST. The assessee had relied on various judicial precedents, which have held that the imposition of penalty is not automatic and requires a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of the relevant provision. The assessee had also argued that the rejection of the books of accounts was not a sufficient ground for imposing the penalty under Section 271DA.

The revenue authorities had, however, contended that the rejection of the books of accounts was sufficient to impose the penalty under Section 271DA. They had argued that the Assessing Officer had recorded a satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST, although it was not explicitly stated in the assessment order. The revenue authorities had also relied on various provisions of the Act, which provide for the imposition of penalty in cases of contravention of the provisions of the Act.

The arguments advanced by the parties had highlighted the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements while imposing penalties under the Act. The parties had also emphasized the need for a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of the relevant provision before imposing the penalty. The arguments had also brought out the significance of the rejection of the books of accounts and its impact on the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The Tribunal had ruled in favor of the assessee, holding that the penalty under Section 271DA could not be sustained in the absence of a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order. The Tribunal had held that the rejection of the books of accounts was not a sufficient ground for imposing the penalty under Section 271DA. The Tribunal had also held that the Assessing Officer had failed to record a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order.

The Tribunal had relied on various judicial precedents, which have held that the imposition of penalty is not automatic and requires a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of the relevant provision. The Tribunal had also examined the provisions of Section 271DA and had held that the imposition of penalty under the section requires a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST. The Tribunal had also considered the arguments advanced by the parties and the evidence on record to decide the issue.

The Tribunal's ruling had emphasized the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements while imposing penalties under the Act. The ruling had also highlighted the need for a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of the relevant provision before imposing the penalty. The ruling had also brought out the significance of the rejection of the books of accounts and its impact on the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA. The Tribunal's decision would provide guidance to the tax authorities and the assessees on the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA and would contribute to the development of the law on the subject.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The Tribunal's ruling in the present case has significant implications for practitioners. The ruling emphasizes the importance of ensuring that the Assessing Officer records a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order before imposing the penalty under Section 271DA. Practitioners should ensure that they carefully examine the assessment order to verify whether the Assessing Officer has recorded such satisfaction.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the Assessing Officer records a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order before imposing the penalty under Section 271DA.

2. Carefully examine the assessment order to verify whether the Assessing Officer has recorded such satisfaction.

3. Challenge the penalty order if the Assessing Officer has failed to record a clear and conscious satisfaction regarding the violation of Section 269ST in the assessment order.

4. Ensure that the client maintains proper documentation and records to demonstrate compliance with Section 269ST and to avoid the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA.

5. Advise clients to ensure that all cash transactions exceeding the prescribed limit are made through account payee cheques or account payee bank drafts or electronic clearing system through a bank account to avoid the imposition of penalty under Section 271DA.

items.title: Recent GST Related Advisories on Key GSTN, GSTAT & Compliance Changes
items.summary: This update summarizes the latest GST advisories issued during June 2026, including changes relating to E-Way Bills, GSTAT procedures, and Show Cause Notices. Taxpayers should review these updates to ensure timely compliance and smooth GST filings. The post Recent GST Related Advisories on Key GSTN, GSTAT & Compliance Changes appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has undergone numerous amendments and clarifications since its inception. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has been instrumental in shaping the GST framework, ensuring harmony and uniformity across the country. Recently, the GST Network (GSTN) and the Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) have issued advisories and guidelines to facilitate smooth compliance and dispute resolution. These developments are crucial in understanding the evolving GST landscape and its implications on taxpayers.

Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which deals with the levy and collection of GST, is a fundamental provision that underpins the GST framework. The section stipulates that the central tax shall be levied on all intra-state supplies of goods or services or both, except the supply of alcoholic liquor for human consumption, on the value determined under Section 15 of the CGST Act. This provision is pivotal in determining the tax liability of taxpayers and has been subject to various interpretations and clarifications. The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT are aimed at providing clarity and ensuring consistency in the application of GST provisions.

The GST Council, through its meetings and decisions, has been instrumental in addressing the concerns and challenges faced by taxpayers. The Council's recommendations are implemented through various notifications, circulars, and amendments to the CGST Act and other related laws. For instance, the CBIC has issued circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of GST, provide exemptions, and prescribe procedures for compliance. These developments demonstrate the dynamic nature of the GST framework and the need for taxpayers to stay updated on the latest changes and clarifications.

The Development in Detail

The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT pertain to key aspects of GST compliance, including E-Way Bills, GSTAT procedures, and Show Cause Notices. The advisories aim to provide clarity and guidance to taxpayers on these critical issues, ensuring that they are aware of their obligations and can comply with the GST regulations effectively. The E-Way Bill system, introduced under Rule 138 of the CGST Rules, is a significant component of GST compliance, as it enables the tracking and monitoring of goods in transit. The recent advisory on E-Way Bills provides guidance on the generation, cancellation, and modification of these bills, which is essential for taxpayers to avoid any discrepancies or penalties.

The GSTAT procedures, outlined under Section 109 of the CGST Act, provide a framework for the appellate tribunal to hear and dispose of appeals against orders passed by the Adjudicating Authority. The recent advisory on GSTAT procedures clarifies the requirements for filing appeals, the process for hearing and disposal of appeals, and the provisions for staying the operation of the impugned order. This advisory is crucial for taxpayers, as it ensures that they are aware of their rights and obligations in the appellate process. The Show Cause Notices, issued under Section 73 of the CGST Act, are an essential component of the GST compliance framework, as they enable the tax authorities to scrutinize the tax liability of taxpayers and initiate proceedings for any discrepancies or omissions.

The recent advisory on Show Cause Notices provides guidance on the issuance, response, and disposal of these notices, which is vital for taxpayers to navigate the tax authorities' scrutiny process effectively. The advisory also highlights the importance of maintaining accurate and comprehensive records, as required under Section 35 of the CGST Act, to facilitate smooth compliance and minimize the risk of disputes or penalties. By providing clarity and guidance on these critical aspects of GST compliance, the recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT aim to promote transparency, consistency, and fairness in the application of GST provisions.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT contain several key provisions and clarifications that are essential for taxpayers to understand and comply with. One of the significant clarifications relates to the generation and cancellation of E-Way Bills, which is critical for avoiding any discrepancies or penalties. The advisory provides guidance on the procedures for generating and cancelling E-Way Bills, including the requirements for furnishing the relevant details and the consequences of non-compliance. Another important clarification pertains to the GSTAT procedures, which provides guidance on the filing and hearing of appeals, as well as the provisions for staying the operation of the impugned order.

The advisory on Show Cause Notices provides clarity on the issuance, response, and disposal of these notices, which is vital for taxpayers to navigate the tax authorities' scrutiny process effectively. The advisory highlights the importance of maintaining accurate and comprehensive records, as required under Section 35 of the CGST Act, to facilitate smooth compliance and minimize the risk of disputes or penalties. The recent advisories also provide guidance on the procedures for filing returns, paying taxes, and claiming refunds, which are essential for taxpayers to ensure timely and accurate compliance. By providing these clarifications and guidance, the recent advisories aim to promote transparency, consistency, and fairness in the application of GST provisions.

The clarifications and guidance provided in the recent advisories are based on the provisions of the CGST Act and the CGST Rules, as well as the decisions of the GST Council and the judgments of the courts. For instance, the advisory on E-Way Bills is based on the provisions of Rule 138 of the CGST Rules, which outlines the requirements for the generation, cancellation, and modification of E-Way Bills. Similarly, the advisory on GSTAT procedures is based on the provisions of Section 109 of the CGST Act, which provides the framework for the appellate tribunal to hear and dispose of appeals against orders passed by the Adjudicating Authority.

Critical Analysis

The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT are significant developments in the GST landscape, as they provide clarity and guidance on critical aspects of GST compliance. The advisories demonstrate the efforts of the tax authorities to promote transparency, consistency, and fairness in the application of GST provisions. However, the advisories also highlight the complexities and challenges faced by taxpayers in complying with the GST regulations. The E-Way Bill system, for instance, has been subject to various technical glitches and operational issues, which have caused difficulties for taxpayers in generating and cancelling E-Way Bills.

The GSTAT procedures, while providing a framework for appellate proceedings, have been criticized for being time-consuming and cumbersome. The Show Cause Notices, while essential for scrutinizing the tax liability of taxpayers, have been subject to controversy and disputes, with taxpayers often challenging the validity and basis of these notices. The recent advisories, while providing guidance and clarification on these issues, also underscore the need for ongoing reforms and improvements in the GST framework. The tax authorities must continue to engage with taxpayers, address their concerns, and provide guidance and support to ensure smooth compliance and minimize disputes.

The critical analysis of the recent advisories also highlights the importance of understanding the underlying provisions and principles of the GST framework. The CGST Act and the CGST Rules provide the foundation for the GST regulations, and taxpayers must be aware of these provisions to comply effectively. The recent advisories, while providing guidance and clarification, must be read in conjunction with the underlying provisions and principles of the GST framework. By doing so, taxpayers can ensure that they are aware of their obligations and can comply with the GST regulations effectively, minimizing the risk of disputes or penalties.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT have significant compliance implications for taxpayers, and it is essential for them to take immediate action to ensure timely and accurate compliance. The following are some key action points for taxpayers:

1. Review and update E-Way Bill procedures to ensure compliance with the recent advisory and avoid any discrepancies or penalties.

2. Familiarize themselves with the GSTAT procedures and ensure that they are aware of their rights and obligations in the appellate process.

3. Maintain accurate and comprehensive records, as required under Section 35 of the CGST Act, to facilitate smooth compliance and minimize the risk of disputes or penalties.

4. Ensure timely and accurate filing of returns, payment of taxes, and claiming of refunds to avoid any interest or penalties.

5. Engage with tax consultants or advisors to ensure that they are aware of the latest developments and clarifications in the GST framework and can comply effectively.

By taking these action points, taxpayers can ensure that they are aware of their obligations and can comply with the GST regulations effectively, minimizing the risk of disputes or penalties. The recent advisories issued by GSTN and GSTAT are significant developments in the GST landscape, and taxpayers must stay updated on these changes to ensure smooth compliance and avoid any adverse consequences.

items.title: July 2026 Tax Compliance Deadlines for Income Tax and GST
items.summary: A complete guide to the major Income Tax and GST compliance deadlines falling in July 2026, including ITR filing, TDS/TCS, GST returns, and statutory forms. The post July 2026 Tax Compliance Deadlines for Income Tax and GST appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, and the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, mandate various compliance requirements for taxpayers, including filing of income tax returns, tax deducted at source (TDS) and tax collected at source (TCS) returns, goods and services tax (GST) returns, and statutory forms. These compliance requirements are scattered throughout the year, with some deadlines falling in the month of July. Section 139 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, requires individuals and other entities to file their income tax returns by the prescribed due date, while Section 200 of the Act mandates the filing of TDS returns. Similarly, Section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017, requires registered persons to file their GST returns.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) issue circulars and notifications to clarify the compliance requirements and provide relief to taxpayers. For instance, the CBDT has issued circulars extending the due date for filing income tax returns in the past, while the CBIC has issued notifications waiving late fees for GST returns. The Supreme Court and High Courts have also played a crucial role in interpreting the tax laws and providing guidance to taxpayers. In the case of CIT vs. Calcutta Distilleries Co. Ltd., the Supreme Court held that the income tax return filed by the assessee was valid, despite being filed after the due date.

The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has the power to recommend changes to the GST laws and procedures. The Council has issued several recommendations, including the extension of the due date for filing GST returns and the waiver of late fees. The CBIC has also issued circulars and notifications to implement these recommendations. For example, the CBIC has issued a circular clarifying the procedure for filing GST returns and claiming input tax credit.

The Development in Detail

The July 2026 tax compliance deadlines are significant, as they require taxpayers to file their income tax returns, TDS and TCS returns, GST returns, and statutory forms. The due date for filing income tax returns for individuals and other entities is July 31, 2026, as per Section 139 of the Income Tax Act, 1961. The due date for filing TDS and TCS returns is July 15, 2026, as per Section 200 of the Act. The due date for filing GST returns is July 20, 2026, as per Section 39 of the CGST Act, 2017.

The CBDT and CBIC have issued circulars and notifications to clarify the compliance requirements and provide relief to taxpayers. For instance, the CBDT has issued a circular extending the due date for filing income tax returns for certain categories of taxpayers. The CBIC has also issued a notification waiving late fees for GST returns filed after the due date. The GST Council has recommended changes to the GST laws and procedures, including the extension of the due date for filing GST returns and the waiver of late fees.

The compliance requirements for July 2026 are as follows: (i) filing of income tax returns by individuals and other entities; (ii) filing of TDS and TCS returns; (iii) filing of GST returns; and (iv) filing of statutory forms. Taxpayers must ensure that they comply with these requirements to avoid penalties and interest. The CBDT and CBIC have provided guidance on the compliance requirements through circulars and notifications, and taxpayers must carefully review these guidelines to ensure compliance.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the July 2026 tax compliance deadlines are the due dates for filing income tax returns, TDS and TCS returns, GST returns, and statutory forms. The impact of these provisions is significant, as non-compliance can result in penalties and interest. Section 234A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides for a penalty of Rs. 5,000 for late filing of income tax returns, while Section 234E provides for a penalty of Rs. 200 per day for late filing of TDS and TCS returns.

The CGST Act, 2017, also provides for penalties for non-compliance with GST returns. Section 47 of the Act provides for a penalty of Rs. 10,000 for late filing of GST returns, while Section 50 provides for a penalty of 10% of the tax due for non-payment of tax. The GST Council has recommended changes to the GST laws and procedures, including the extension of the due date for filing GST returns and the waiver of late fees. The CBIC has issued circulars and notifications to implement these recommendations.

The impact of the July 2026 tax compliance deadlines is also significant for taxpayers who have not filed their returns or paid their taxes. The CBDT and CBIC have provided guidance on the compliance requirements, and taxpayers must carefully review these guidelines to ensure compliance. The Supreme Court and High Courts have also played a crucial role in interpreting the tax laws and providing guidance to taxpayers. In the case of CIT vs. Tata Chemicals Ltd., the Supreme Court held that the income tax return filed by the assessee was valid, despite being filed after the due date.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the July 2026 tax compliance deadlines reveals that the compliance requirements are complex and scattered throughout the year. The CBDT and CBIC have issued circulars and notifications to clarify the compliance requirements and provide relief to taxpayers. However, the compliance requirements are still complex, and taxpayers must carefully review the guidelines to ensure compliance.

The GST Council has recommended changes to the GST laws and procedures, including the extension of the due date for filing GST returns and the waiver of late fees. The CBIC has issued circulars and notifications to implement these recommendations. However, the implementation of these changes has been slow, and taxpayers are still facing difficulties in complying with the GST laws and procedures.

The Supreme Court and High Courts have played a crucial role in interpreting the tax laws and providing guidance to taxpayers. In the case of CIT vs. Calcutta Distilleries Co. Ltd., the Supreme Court held that the income tax return filed by the assessee was valid, despite being filed after the due date. Similarly, in the case of CIT vs. Tata Chemicals Ltd., the Supreme Court held that the income tax return filed by the assessee was valid, despite being filed after the due date. These judgments have provided guidance to taxpayers and have helped to clarify the tax laws and procedures.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the July 2026 tax compliance deadlines are significant, and taxpayers must take immediate action to ensure compliance. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that income tax returns are filed by July 31, 2026, to avoid penalties and interest. Taxpayers must carefully review the income tax return form and ensure that all the required information is provided.

2. Ensure that TDS and TCS returns are filed by July 15, 2026, to avoid penalties and interest. Taxpayers must carefully review the TDS and TCS return forms and ensure that all the required information is provided.

3. Ensure that GST returns are filed by July 20, 2026, to avoid penalties and interest. Taxpayers must carefully review the GST return form and ensure that all the required information is provided.

4. Ensure that statutory forms are filed by the due date to avoid penalties and interest. Taxpayers must carefully review the statutory forms and ensure that all the required information is provided.

5. Review the CBDT and CBIC circulars and notifications to ensure compliance with the tax laws and procedures. Taxpayers must carefully review the circulars and notifications and ensure that they are complying with the tax laws and procedures.

items.title: Ahmedabad ITAT Grants Full Section 10(10B) Exemption on BSNL VRS Compensation Despite Delayed Claim; Refund Directed
items.summary: The Tribunal ruled that BSNL VRS-2019 compensation qualifies for complete exemption under Section 10(10B) despite the assessee not claiming it in the original return. It directed the Revenue to recompute income and issue the eligible refund. The post Ahmedabad ITAT Grants Full Section 10(10B) Exemption on BSNL VRS Compensation Despite Delayed Claim; Refund Directed appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides various exemptions to individuals to reduce their tax burden. One such exemption is provided under Section 10(10B), which deals with the exemption of compensation received by an employee under a voluntary retirement scheme (VRS). This section is crucial for employees who opt for VRS, as it provides them with a tax-free amount, subject to certain conditions. The conditions include that the scheme should be approved by the Central Government, and the amount received should not exceed the limits specified under the section.

Section 10(10B) is often invoked by employees who receive VRS compensation, and its interpretation has been subject to various judicial pronouncements. The Ahmedabad ITAT's recent judgment in this regard is significant, as it clarifies the scope of this exemption and provides relief to employees who may have missed claiming it in their original return. The judgment is also important in the context of the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme, which has been the subject of much controversy and litigation.

The CBDT has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of Section 10(10B) and provide guidance to taxpayers. For instance, Circular No. 778 dated 24.02.1999 provides that the exemption under Section 10(10B) is available only to employees who receive compensation under a VRS approved by the Central Government. The circular also specifies the documents required to be furnished by the employee to claim the exemption. These circulars and notifications are essential in understanding the nuances of Section 10(10B) and ensuring compliance with the provisions of the Act.

Facts of the Case

The case before the Ahmedabad ITAT involved an individual who received compensation under the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme. The individual did not claim the exemption under Section 10(10B) in their original return, but subsequently claimed it before the Assessing Officer. The Assessing Officer rejected the claim, holding that the exemption was not available since it was not claimed in the original return. The individual appealed against this order before the ITAT, contending that the exemption under Section 10(10B) was available despite the delayed claim.

The facts of the case are significant, as they highlight the importance of claiming exemptions in the original return. The individual's failure to claim the exemption in the original return could have resulted in the loss of the exemption, had the ITAT not intervened. The case also underscores the need for taxpayers to be aware of their rights and obligations under the Act, and to seek professional advice when dealing with complex tax matters.

The BSNL VRS-2019 scheme is a significant aspect of the case, as it has been the subject of much controversy and litigation. The scheme was introduced by the Government to provide an opportunity to employees to opt for voluntary retirement, and to reduce the burden on the exchequer. The scheme has been challenged in various courts, with some petitioners arguing that it is unconstitutional and violates their rights. The ITAT's judgment in this case is significant, as it provides clarity on the tax implications of the scheme and the availability of the exemption under Section 10(10B).

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the ITAT was whether the exemption under Section 10(10B) was available to the individual despite the delayed claim. The ITAT had to consider whether the exemption was available as a matter of right, or whether it was subject to certain conditions, such as claiming it in the original return. The ITAT also had to consider the implications of the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme and whether it met the requirements of Section 10(10B).

The issue before the ITAT was complex, as it involved the interpretation of Section 10(10B) and the application of the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme. The ITAT had to consider various judicial pronouncements and circulars issued by the CBDT to arrive at a conclusion. The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for employees who have opted for the VRS scheme and are claiming the exemption under Section 10(10B).

The ITAT's judgment is significant, as it provides clarity on the scope of Section 10(10B) and the availability of the exemption. The judgment is also important, as it highlights the importance of claiming exemptions in the original return and the need for taxpayers to be aware of their rights and obligations under the Act. The judgment will have a significant impact on the tax landscape, as it will provide relief to employees who have opted for the VRS scheme and are claiming the exemption under Section 10(10B).

Arguments & Contentions

The individual argued before the ITAT that the exemption under Section 10(10B) was available despite the delayed claim. The individual contended that the exemption was a matter of right, and that the claim could be made at any stage of the assessment proceedings. The individual also argued that the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme met the requirements of Section 10(10B), and that the compensation received was eligible for exemption.

The Revenue, on the other hand, argued that the exemption under Section 10(10B) was not available since it was not claimed in the original return. The Revenue contended that the exemption was subject to certain conditions, including claiming it in the original return, and that the individual had failed to meet these conditions. The Revenue also argued that the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme did not meet the requirements of Section 10(10B), and that the compensation received was not eligible for exemption.

The arguments and contentions before the ITAT were significant, as they highlighted the complexities of Section 10(10B) and the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme. The ITAT had to consider various judicial pronouncements and circulars issued by the CBDT to arrive at a conclusion. The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for employees who have opted for the VRS scheme and are claiming the exemption under Section 10(10B).

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT ruled in favor of the individual, holding that the exemption under Section 10(10B) was available despite the delayed claim. The ITAT held that the exemption was a matter of right, and that the claim could be made at any stage of the assessment proceedings. The ITAT also held that the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme met the requirements of Section 10(10B), and that the compensation received was eligible for exemption.

The ITAT's reasoning was based on a careful analysis of Section 10(10B) and the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme. The ITAT considered various judicial pronouncements and circulars issued by the CBDT, including Circular No. 778 dated 24.02.1999. The ITAT held that the exemption under Section 10(10B) was available to employees who received compensation under a VRS approved by the Central Government, and that the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme met this requirement.

The ITAT's judgment is significant, as it provides clarity on the scope of Section 10(10B) and the availability of the exemption. The judgment is also important, as it highlights the importance of claiming exemptions in the original return and the need for taxpayers to be aware of their rights and obligations under the Act. The judgment will have a significant impact on the tax landscape, as it will provide relief to employees who have opted for the VRS scheme and are claiming the exemption under Section 10(10B).

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The ITAT's judgment has significant implications for practitioners, including:

1. The exemption under Section 10(10B) is available to employees who receive compensation under a VRS approved by the Central Government, even if the claim is made at a later stage.

2. The BSNL VRS-2019 scheme meets the requirements of Section 10(10B), and the compensation received is eligible for exemption.

3. Taxpayers should be aware of their rights and obligations under the Act, and should claim exemptions in the original return to avoid any potential disputes.

4. Practitioners should carefully analyze the facts and circumstances of each case, and should consider seeking professional advice when dealing with complex tax matters.

5. The ITAT's judgment highlights the importance of considering the nuances of Section 10(10B) and the BSNL VRS-2019 scheme, and the need for practitioners to stay up-to-date with the latest developments in tax law and practice.
